items.front: En route strip: where do aircraft count, heavy indicator, type, and equipment suffix go?
items.back: Block 4. It is the combined block for number of aircraft, H/ if appropriate, aircraft type, and equipment suffix.

items.front: En route strip: which block records aircraft identification?
items.back: Block 3 records aircraft identification.

items.front: En route strip: which block records altitude information?
items.back: Block 20 records altitude information in hundreds of feet unless local authorization allows thousands-format notation.

items.front: En route strip: what is block 20a for?
items.back: Reported RA events. Record RA, a climb/descent arrow if that action is reported, and the time the event is reported when block 20a use is required.

items.front: Clearance abbreviation: N
items.back: Clearance not delivered.

items.front: Clearance abbreviation: PD
items.back: Cleared to climb or descend at pilot's discretion.

items.front: Miscellaneous abbreviation: RA
items.back: Resolution advisory, meaning a pilot-reported TCAS event.

items.front: Miscellaneous abbreviations: RP vs RX
items.back: RP means report immediately upon passing a fix or altitude. RX means report crossing.

items.front: ALS intensity: day visibility less than 1 mile
items.back: Step 5.

items.front: ALS intensity: day visibility 3 to but not including 5 miles
items.back: Step 3.

items.front: ALS intensity: night visibility greater than 3 miles
items.back: Step 1.

items.front: HIRL/RCLS/TDZL intensity: night visibility less than 1 mile
items.back: Step 4.

items.front: HIRL/RCLS/TDZL intensity: night visibility 3 to 5 miles inclusive
items.back: Step 2.

items.front: HIRL/RCLS/TDZL intensity: night visibility more than 5 miles
items.back: Step 1.

items.front: Radar separation application: where do you decode aircraft equipment suffix capability?
items.back: Use TBL 2-3-10, Aircraft Equipment Suffixes, before relying on equipment-dependent navigation or radar-separation capability.

items.front: Radar separation application: what is the operational role of the equipment suffix reference?
items.back: It supports determining whether the aircraft has the equipment capability needed for the procedure being applied; do not treat the suffix as decoration.

items.front: Radar departures: successive same-runway departures, 15 degrees or more immediately after departure
items.back: 1 mile if radar identification will be established within 1 mile of the runway end and wake turbulence separation is not required.

items.front: RNAV SID same-runway successive departures: 10 degrees or more immediately after departure
items.back: 1 mile, if wake turbulence separation is not required.

items.front: RNAV SID parallel departures: divergence no later than 8 NM from the further DER
items.back: Minimum centerline separation 1,130 feet; release-distance stagger 3,000 feet.

items.front: RNAV SID parallel departures: immediate divergence from the further DER
items.back: Minimum centerline separation 700 feet; release-distance stagger 1,000 feet.

items.front: Approach course interception: less than 2 miles from interception point to approach gate
items.back: Maximum interception angle is 20 degrees. The same 20-degree limit applies when triple simultaneous approaches are in use.

items.front: Approach course interception: 2 miles or more from interception point to approach gate
items.back: Maximum interception angle is 30 degrees, or 45 degrees for helicopters.

items.front: Lateral separation: protected airspace values of 50 miles and 10 miles
items.back: Add the protected airspace values. The applicable lateral separation minimum is 60 miles.

items.front: Lateral separation at an oceanic boundary entering airspace with a larger minimum
items.back: The smaller separation must exist at the boundary, and flight paths must diverge by 15 degrees or more until the larger minimum is established.

items.front: Lateral separation: protected airspace overlap at an intersection
items.back: Use the protected-airspace sum at the relevant point of overlap to determine when lateral separation exists or when vertical separation is still needed.

items.front: VOR/VORTAC/TACAN divergence-distance minima: 26-35 degrees, FL 240 through FL 450
items.back: 13 miles.

items.front: VOR/VORTAC/TACAN divergence-distance minima: 36-90 degrees, FL 230 and below
items.back: 8 miles.

items.front: NDB divergence-distance minima: 75 degrees, FL 230 and below
items.back: 7 miles.

items.front: NDB divergence-distance minima: 90 degrees, FL 240 through FL 450
items.back: 7 miles.

items.front: Pacific ADS-C criteria: 30 NM with RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180
items.back: Maximum ADS-C periodic reporting interval is 10 minutes.

items.front: Pacific ADS-C criteria: 20 NM with RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180
items.back: Maximum ADS-C periodic reporting interval is 192 seconds.

items.front: Caribbean ADS-C criteria: 50 NM with RNP 10, RCP 240, RSP 180
items.back: Maximum ADS-C periodic reporting interval is 27 minutes.

items.front: Caribbean ADS-C criteria: 50 NM with RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180
items.back: Maximum ADS-C periodic reporting interval is 32 minutes.

items.front: North Atlantic ADS-C criteria: 30 NM with RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180
items.back: Maximum ADS-C periodic reporting interval is 10 minutes.

items.front: North Atlantic ADS-C criteria: 20 NM with RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180
items.back: Maximum ADS-C periodic reporting interval is 192 seconds.
